question: [File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: parseLagByCluster()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence Prometheus responses inject many series entries with the same cluster label, exploiting the merge function (_, b) -> b at line 113 to ensure the last-seen value wins, and by controlling the ordering of series in the response, override the legitimate lag value for localCluster with a crafted low value, causing evaluate() to return UP?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: evaluate()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence Prometheus responses inject a series for localCluster with a lag value just above thresholdSeconds, while injecting all other-cluster series with lag values just above (localLag + thresholdSeconds), so that isOtherClearlyBetter() returns false and the monitor stays UP, keeping clients on a lagging cluster serving stale transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 5-second timeout in ImporterLagHealthProperties to cause the Prometheus query to consistently time out by introducing artificial latency, so that health() always catches the timeout exception and returns UP, permanently masking importer lag and preventing failover to a cluster with accurate transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user who can perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the unencrypted HTTP connection to Prometheus (since PrometheusApiClient uses SimpleClientHttpRequestFactory with no TLS enforcement) inject a crafted response that reports near-zero lag for the local cluster, causing health() to return UP and hiding real importer lag?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: evaluate()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence Prometheus responses inject a series where the cluster label exactly matches localCluster but the lag value is a very large finite double (e.g., Double.MAX_VALUE), causing isLagAboveThreshold() to return true, but then inject all other-cluster series with lag values of (Double.MAX_VALUE - thresholdSeconds + 1), causing isOtherClearlyBetter() to return false and the monitor to stay UP despite extreme local lag?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: parseDouble()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence Prometheus responses inject a series where value.get(1) is a numeric string representing a negative lag value (e.g., '-100.0'), which passes parseDouble() and Double.isFinite() but fails the lagSeconds > thresholdSeconds check in isLagAboveThreshold() at line 92, causing the local cluster to always appear healthy regardless of actual lag?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: evaluate()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence Prometheus responses inject a series for localCluster with a lag value just above thresholdSeconds, then remove all other-cluster series, causing bestOtherLag() to return OptionalDouble.empty(), isOtherClearlyBetter() to return false, and the monitor to return UP even when the local cluster is lagging?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that health() is called periodically by Spring Boot Actuator and the result is cached, by timing a Prometheus response injection to coincide with the health check poll interval, so that a single crafted response causes the monitor to report UP for the entire cache duration, masking real importer lag?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: evaluate()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence Prometheus responses cause a race condition by injecting a low-lag value for localCluster during one health check cycle and a high-lag value during the next, creating oscillating UP/DOWN signals that prevent stable failover decisions and keep clients intermittently reading from a lagging cluster?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/ImporterLagHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: evaluate()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence Prometheus responses repeatedly toggle the localCluster lag value above and below thresholdSeconds, causing the health check to oscillate between UP and DOWN, triggering repeated failover events that cause clients to see inconsistent transaction history across different clusters?
